== name in kana (P1814) == * しおゆひこじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 1410005005027 == has part(s) (P527) == * {{ill|Shioyuhiko Shrine okumiya|lt=Shioyuhiko Shrine okumiya|WD=Q135194471|1=Shioyuhiko Shrine okumiya}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '秋田県横手市山内大松川字御嶽山3', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:塩湯彦神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11428793}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Shioyuhiko Shrine |画像={{画像募集中|cat=横手市}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Akita Prefecture|Q81863|WD=Q81863|1=Akita Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yokote|Q496479|WD=Q496479|1=Yokote, Akita}}3 Ontakeyama, Oomatsukawa, Sannai |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601|1=672}} ({{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 12) |例祭=3rd Sunday of June |地図= {{Location map|Japan Akita|label=Shioyuhiko Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=240}} }} '''Shioyuhiko Shrine''' (しおゆひこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yokote|Q496479|WD=Q496479|1=Yokote, Akita}}, {{ill|lt=Akita Prefecture|Q81863|WD=Q81863|1=Akita Prefecture}}Hisae Otomo, "Shioyuhiko Shrine," 'Akita Daihyakka Jiten,' Akita Sakigake Shinpo, 1981, ISBN 4870200074. It is a {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and was formerly ranked {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Origin === According to shrine tradition, it was founded by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=En no Gyōja|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601|1=672}} ({{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 12). It appears in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' as "Shioyuhiko Shrine, Hiraka District, Dewa Province," i.e., a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}[https://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/yokote/043_sioyuhiko.html "Shioyuhiko Shrine" official site of Akita Prefecture Shrine Office]. It seems originally to have been a hot-spring deity of Mount Ontake rising east of the Hiraka basin, but under influence of {{ill|lt=Kumano Sanzan|Q10403700|WD=Q10403700|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano Three Shrines}}-line {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} it came to be called "Kumano-dō" in the medieval period. Likely for that reason, Hayatama-no-Mikoto is enshrined. After a period of decline, the Otomo family, priests of {{ill|lt=Horowasan Haushiwake Shrine|Q11385699|WD=Q11385699|1=Horowasan Haushiwake Shrine}} (another Shikinaisha in the same district), served as {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274|1=Bettō}}, while the Minashi family in charge of kagura managed it in practice. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when {{ill|lt=Satake Yoshinori|Q8514533|WD=Q8514533|1=Satake Yoshitada}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Kubota Domain|Q1067720|WD=Q1067720|lt=Akita Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}, restored old shrines within the domain, this shrine was also restored, and it was revered through the Edo period as one of Akita Domain's Three Province Shrines. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5), it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In {{ill|lt=1963|Q2755|WD=Q2755|1=1963}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 38), heavy snow caused the shrine buildings to collapse, and ritual functions shifted to the satomiya; in {{ill|lt=1982|Q2436|WD=Q2436|1=1982}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 57), it was rebuilt by local supporters[http://www.city.yokote.lg.jp/kanko/page000059.html "Shioyuhiko Shrine (Sannai)" official site of {{ill|lt=Yokote|Q496479|WD=Q496479|1=Yokote, Akita}}]. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727|1=July 21}}: annual festival === Outside/associated shrines === * '''Satomiya''': '''Shioyuhiko Tsurugaike Shrine''' (Tsurugaike, Tsuchibuchi, Sannai, Yokote) * '''Inner sanctuary''': '''Shirataki Kannon''' (Kannonzawa, Oomatsukawa, Sannai, Yokote) === Scenic / historic points === * The foothills of Mount Ontake where this shrine stands are the northern limit of large clusters of {{ill|lt=yuki-tsubaki|Q11345564|WD=Q11345564|1=Camellia rusticana}}. * Around the satomiya, Tsurugaike Park is developed, and hot springs are nearby. === Timeline === < > indicates related events * {{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601|1=672}} ({{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 12): foundation * {{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 7): <{{ill|lt=Satake Yoshinobu (Ukyō-no-daibu)|Q1071525|WD=Q1071525|lt=Satake Yoshinobu|1=Satake Yoshinobu}} enters Akita.> * {{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|WD=Q6884|1=1715}} ({{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 5): rebuilding[http://www.akitafan.com/sightseeing/detail.html?data_id=1550 "Shioyuhiko Shrine" AkitaFan.com (Akita Tourism Federation)] * {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 1) March: <{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri Edict|1=UNKNOWN}}> * {{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}} (Meiji 4) May 14: * {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5): ranked {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} === Other === Possibly due to faith around Shirataki Kannon, treated as this shrine's inner sanctuary, it is unusual for a {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} mountain in that women were not prohibited from old times. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}}

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=240}} }} '''Shioyuhiko Shrine''' (しおゆひこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yokote|Q496479|WD=Q496479|1=Yokote, Akita}}, {{ill|lt=Akita Prefecture|Q81863|WD=Q81863|1=Akita Prefecture}}Hisae Otomo, "Shioyuhiko Shrine," 'Akita Daihyakka Jiten,' Akita Sakigake Shinpo, 1981, ISBN 4870200074. It is a {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and was formerly ranked {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Origin === According to shrine tradition, it was founded by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=En no Gyōja|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601|1=672}} ({{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 12). It appears in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' as "Shioyuhiko Shrine, Hiraka District, Dewa Province," i.e., a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}[https://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/yokote/043_sioyuhiko.html "Shioyuhiko Shrine" official site of Akita Prefecture Shrine Office]. It seems originally to have been a hot-spring deity of Mount Ontake rising east of the Hiraka basin, but under influence of {{ill|lt=Kumano Sanzan|Q10403700|WD=Q10403700|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano Three Shrines}}-line {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} it came to be called "Kumano-dō" in the medieval period. Likely for that reason, Hayatama-no-Mikoto is enshrined. After a period of decline, the Otomo family, priests of {{ill|lt=Horowasan Haushiwake Shrine|Q11385699|WD=Q11385699|1=Horowasan Haushiwake Shrine}} (another Shikinaisha in the same district), served as {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274|1=Bettō}}, while the Minashi family in charge of kagura managed it in practice. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when {{ill|lt=Satake Yoshinori|Q8514533|WD=Q8514533|1=Satake Yoshitada}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Kubota Domain|Q1067720|WD=Q1067720|lt=Akita Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}, restored old shrines within the domain, this shrine was also restored, and it was revered through the Edo period as one of Akita Domain's Three Province Shrines. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5), it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In {{ill|lt=1963|Q2755|WD=Q2755|1=1963}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 38), heavy snow caused the shrine buildings to collapse, and ritual functions shifted to the satomiya; in {{ill|lt=1982|Q2436|WD=Q2436|1=1982}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 57), it was rebuilt by local supporters[http://www.city.yokote.lg.jp/kanko/page000059.html "Shioyuhiko Shrine (Sannai)" official site of {{ill|lt=Yokote|Q496479|WD=Q496479|1=Yokote, Akita}}]. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727|1=July 21}}: annual festival === Outside/associated shrines === * '''Satomiya''': '''Shioyuhiko Tsurugaike Shrine''' (Tsurugaike, Tsuchibuchi, Sannai, Yokote) * '''Inner sanctuary''': '''Shirataki Kannon''' (Kannonzawa, Oomatsukawa, Sannai, Yokote) === Scenic / historic points === * The foothills of Mount Ontake where this shrine stands are the northern limit of large clusters of {{ill|lt=yuki-tsubaki|Q11345564|WD=Q11345564|1=Camellia rusticana}}. * Around the satomiya, Tsurugaike Park is developed, and hot springs are nearby. === Timeline === < > indicates related events * <{{ill|lt=Satake Yoshinobu (Ukyō-no-daibu)|Q1071525|WD=Q1071525|lt=Satake Yoshinobu|1=Satake Yoshinobu}} enters Akita.> * {{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|WD=Q6884|1=1715}} ({{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 5): rebuilding[http://www.akitafan.com/sightseeing/detail.html?data_id=1550 "Shioyuhiko Shrine" AkitaFan.com (Akita Tourism Federation)] * {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 1) March: <{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri Edict|1=UNKNOWN}}> * {{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}} (Meiji 4) May 14: * {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5): ranked {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} === Other === Possibly due to faith around Shirataki Kannon, treated as this shrine's inner sanctuary, it is unusual for a {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} mountain in that women were not prohibited from old times.
